worth_it_explanation: Reporting accuracy decides whether the revenue and fill numbers a publisher reads in FreeWheel match the money that actually arrives and the impressions that actually served. When reported revenue drifts from remittance, or when timezones and dimensions are configured so two systems count the same days differently, the publisher makes pricing and packaging decisions on numbers that do not reconcile. This check is a review of the reconciliation a publisher already has against the configuration of the reports themselves, so it costs reading and matching time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete gap between what the report shows and what the payment confirms.
intro: FreeWheel reporting is where a publisher reads the revenue, fill, and impression counts that drive pricing, packaging, and demand decisions. Those numbers only hold their weight when they reconcile against the payments that arrive and against the other systems that count the same inventory. This subcategory is about reporting accuracy itself, whether reported revenue and fill line up with actual remittance and whether report dimensions and timezones are set so the numbers stay comparable across systems, rather than the ad serving mechanics that produce the impressions. These checks confirm that the reports a publisher relies on are reconciled and configured for apples-to-apples comparison rather than accepted at face value.

audit_checks.title: Reported revenue and fill are not reconciled against actual remittance
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Reported revenue in an ad server is an estimate of what inventory earned, and the money that arrives from buyers and partners can differ from it because of discrepancies, deductions, and post-serving adjustments. When a publisher never reconciles the reported figure against the remittance, a persistent gap between the two can sit unnoticed, so revenue planning and the value placed on inventory rest on a number that the payments do not confirm.

educate.base:
FreeWheel reports the revenue and fill a publisher's video and Connected TV (CTV) inventory generated, and that report is the publisher's working view of what the inventory earned. The figure is an ad server's accounting of impressions served and the prices attached to them. The money that actually arrives, the remittance from direct-sold buyers and from programmatic partners, is a separate measurement, and the two do not always match.

The gap between reported revenue and remittance has ordinary causes. A buyer's own count of impressions can differ from the seller's count, which is the discrepancy that measurement standards exist to bound. Programmatic partners deduct fees and apply post-serving adjustments before they pay. Make-goods, cancellations, and billing corrections land after the report was first read. None of these means the report is wrong, but each one moves the number that pays from the number the report showed, and a publisher who only ever reads the report has no way to see how far apart they sit.

Reconciliation is the practice of matching the reported figure against the remittance on a regular cycle and accounting for the difference. A reconciled setup carries a record that, for a given period, reported revenue was compared against what was paid, the gap was quantified, and the causes were attributed to discrepancy, fees, or adjustments rather than left as an unexplained shortfall. An unreconciled setup reads the FreeWheel report as the revenue figure and treats whatever arrives as a separate matter, so a structural gap can persist across periods without anyone naming it.

A few signs point to reported revenue and fill being read rather than reconciled:
  • No regular cycle exists where reported FreeWheel revenue is matched against direct-sold invoices and programmatic partner statements
  • A recurring difference between reported and paid revenue has no record attributing it to discrepancy, fees, or adjustments
  • Fill or impression counts in FreeWheel are not compared against the counts a partner or a third-party measurement vendor reports for the same inventory
You may want to confirm that reported revenue and fill are reconciled against what is actually paid on a regular cycle. One approach is to match a recent period's FreeWheel report against the remittance for the same period, quantify the gap, and attribute it before treating either number as settled.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a streaming or broadcast property, revenue often arrives through a mix of direct-sold deals and programmatic demand, and each path settles on its own terms and timing. You may want to reconcile the FreeWheel report against direct-sold invoices and programmatic statements separately, so a gap in one path is not hidden by the other inside a blended total.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content video property, sponsored video and shoppable ad placements can carry negotiated rates and make-good terms that adjust revenue after the report is first read. You may want to reconcile reported revenue against the settled invoices for those placements, so post-serving adjustments on negotiated deals are accounted for rather than left as an unexplained gap.

educate.learn_more_note: FreeWheel's detailed reconciliation and revenue reporting documentation is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review whether a regular reconciliation cycle matches reported FreeWheel revenue and fill against the remittance and the counts other systems report, and look for a record of the gap and its causes.

freewheel_reporting.label: FreeWheel reporting
steps: Build a revenue and impressions report for a recent closed period, broken out by demand source so direct-sold and programmatic revenue are separable.
Record the reported revenue, impressions, and fill rate for that period as the baseline to reconcile against.
Ask the publisher for the remittance covering the same period, the direct-sold invoices and the programmatic partner statements, and record what was actually paid.
Compare reported revenue against the remittance, quantify the gap, and record whether the difference is attributed to count discrepancy, partner fees, or post-serving adjustments or left unexplained.

partner_statements.label: Partner and third-party statements
steps: Collect the impression or revenue counts that a programmatic partner or a third-party measurement vendor reports for the same inventory and period.
Compare those counts against the FreeWheel figures to see how far the two systems diverge.
Note whether any divergence is within the range measurement standards treat as acceptable or whether it is large enough to point to a configuration or counting problem.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for a recent period, the reported FreeWheel revenue and fill, the remittance actually paid, and any third-party counts for the same inventory, along with whether a regular reconciliation cycle and a record of the gap exist. A setup where reported figures are matched against remittance on a regular cycle and the difference is quantified and attributed passes. A setup where the report is read as the revenue figure with no reconciliation against what was paid, or with a recurring unexplained gap, is a fail recorded with the period and the size of the gap.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the reported revenue and fill, the remittance for the same period, and whether a reconciliation record exists above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} reads FreeWheel reported revenue and fill without reconciling them against the remittance that actually arrives. Reported revenue is an ad server's estimate of what inventory earned, and the money paid can differ from it because of count discrepancies, partner fees, and post-serving adjustments, so a structural gap between the two can persist unnoticed. {{voice_recommend}} matching a recent period's FreeWheel report against the direct-sold invoices and programmatic statements for the same period, quantifying the gap, and attributing it to discrepancy, fees, or adjustments. After the first reconciliation, keep the match on a regular cycle so the reported figure and the paid figure stay accounted for against each other.

audit_checks.title: Report timezones and dimensions are configured so the numbers are not comparable across systems
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When a FreeWheel report and the system it is compared against use different timezones or count along different dimensions, the same inventory lands in different days and different buckets, so the totals diverge even when no real discrepancy exists. A publisher reading two reports that were never set up to be comparable can chase a gap that is an artifact of configuration rather than a problem with the inventory or the payment.

educate.base:
Two reports only compare cleanly when they count the same things over the same window. A FreeWheel report and a programmatic partner's report, or a FreeWheel report and an internal finance system, each carry their own timezone setting and their own choice of dimensions to break the numbers out by. When those settings differ, the reports describe the same inventory in terms that do not line up, and the totals diverge for reasons that have nothing to do with the inventory itself.

Timezone is the most direct example. A report built in one timezone closes its day at a different moment than a report built in another, so impressions served near midnight fall into one day in the first report and the next day in the second. Compare a single day across the two and the counts will not match, not because impressions went missing but because the boundary of the day moved. The gap is an artifact of the timezone setting, and it disappears when both reports are aligned to the same zone over a window long enough to absorb the edge.

Dimensions create the same problem in a different shape. One report may count by the demand source that served the impression while another counts by the placement or the deal, and a report that aggregates at one level cannot be matched line by line against a report that aggregates at another. Currency adds a third axis since a report settled in one currency cannot be compared against a figure in another without a stated conversion. Comparable reporting means the timezone, the dimensions, and the currency are set so the two views describe the inventory the same way before any gap between them is treated as real.

A few signs point to reports that are not configured for comparison:
  • The FreeWheel report and the partner or finance report it is checked against run in different timezones with no alignment step before they are compared
  • The two reports aggregate along different dimensions, so totals are matched at levels that do not correspond
  • Revenue figures in different currencies are compared without a stated conversion rate and date
You may want to confirm that the reports being compared share a timezone, comparable dimensions, and a stated currency basis. One approach is to align both reports to the same timezone and dimension breakout over a multi-day window before reading any difference between them as a real discrepancy.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a streaming or broadcast property serving a national or international audience, FreeWheel and a demand partner can sit in different timezones by default, and the day boundary shift shows up most on high-volume evening viewing. You may want to align both reports to a single timezone over a multi-day window so prime-time impressions near midnight are counted in the same day on both sides.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content video property, revenue may be reported in more than one currency across sponsored placements and programmatic demand, and finance may settle in a single reporting currency. You may want to fix a stated conversion rate and date for each comparison so a currency difference is not read as a revenue gap.

educate.learn_more_note: FreeWheel's report builder settings for timezone, dimensions, and currency are documented in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the timezone, dimension, and currency settings on the FreeWheel report and on each report it is compared against to see whether they are aligned for an apples-to-apples comparison.

freewheel_reporting.label: FreeWheel reporting
steps: Open the report being used to read revenue and fill and record its timezone setting, the dimensions it breaks out by, and the currency it reports in.
Build the same report aligned to the timezone and dimension breakout used by the system it will be compared against, over a multi-day window rather than a single day.
Record whether the report's default settings match the comparison system or whether an alignment step is needed before the two are read against each other.

comparison_reports.label: Comparison reports
steps: Pull the partner statement or finance report that the FreeWheel figure is checked against and record its timezone, dimensions, and currency.
Compare those settings against the FreeWheel report's settings and note every axis where they differ.
Where currencies differ, record the conversion rate and date applied, and confirm the same basis is used on both sides of the comparison.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the timezone, dimensions, and currency of the FreeWheel report and of each report it is compared against. A setup where the compared reports share a timezone, comparable dimensions, and a stated currency basis, or where a documented alignment step reconciles them, passes. A setup where reports in different timezones, dimensions, or currencies are compared with no alignment is a fail recorded with the axes that do not match and the comparison they affect.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the timezone, dimensions, and currency of each report being compared above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} compares FreeWheel reports against other systems that use a different timezone, different dimensions, or a different currency, so the numbers are not configured to be comparable. When the day boundary, the breakout level, or the currency basis differs, the same inventory lands in different buckets and the totals diverge for reasons that are configuration artifacts rather than real discrepancies. {{voice_recommend}} aligning the compared reports to a single timezone and a comparable dimension breakout over a multi-day window, and fixing a stated conversion rate and date wherever currencies differ. After the alignment, treat any remaining gap as a real discrepancy worth investigating rather than an artifact of mismatched settings.
